era_context: The early 14th century CE spans the late Medieval period in Europe, the height of the Mali Empire in West Africa, the Yuan Dynasty in China under Mongol rule, and the Ottoman Empire's early expansion in Anatolia. This is an era of long-distance trade (Silk Road, trans-Saharan routes), religious ferment (Islamic expansion, Christian Crusades waning, Avignon Papacy), and significant technological diffusion (gunpowder weapons spreading westward, maritime innovations in the Mediterranean and Indian Ocean).
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa beyond Mali (limited written sources)
Southeast Asia and the Pacific (sparse documentation)
Northern Europe and Scandinavia (limited contemporary records)
Americas (no written records; archaeological evidence minimal for this specific year)

events.title: Mansa Musa's Hajj to Mecca (Alternative Dating)
events.region: Mali Empire, West Africa; Egypt; Arabia
events.description: Some sources date Mansa Musa's pilgrimage to Mecca to 1323 CE rather than 1324 CE. The Islamic calendar conversion (Hijri to Gregorian) creates ambiguity; the hajj likely occurred in Hijri year 724 AH, which corresponds to approximately March 1323 – March 1324 CE. The event itself is the same as documented in 1324: a massive caravan of approximately 60,000 people, including 12,000 slaves carrying gold, traveled to Mecca and passed through Cairo, where Musa's spending caused economic disruption.
key_figures: Caliph al-Nasir Muhammad (Mamluk Sultan of Egypt)

sources.name: Al-Umari, Masalik al-absar fi mamalik al-amsar
Islamic calendar conversion tables and historiographic consensus

events.certainty_note: The hajj is confirmed, but the precise Gregorian year is ambiguous due to Islamic calendar conversion. Modern scholars debate whether it occurred in 1323 or 1324 CE.
cross_references: Part of Mali's rise to prominence

events.title: Papal Schism and Avignon Papacy Consolidation
events.description: Pope John XXII (Jacques d'Euse) was consolidating papal authority from Avignon, France, rather than Rome. The Avignon Papacy (1309-1377) represented a shift in ecclesiastical power and was controversial among European Christendom. In 1323, John XXII was engaged in theological disputes (particularly regarding poverty and the nature of Christ) and was strengthening papal administrative structures. This period saw the papacy become increasingly entangled with French royal politics.
events.certainty_note: The Avignon Papacy and John XXII's reign are well-documented in papal records and contemporary chronicles.
cross_references: Related to broader decline of papal temporal power

events.title: Scottish Wars of Independence — Robert the Bruce's Consolidation
events.description: Robert the Bruce, King of Scotland, was consolidating Scottish independence following the Battle of Bannockburn (1314). By 1323, Bruce had secured most of Scotland and was negotiating with England. The Declaration of Arbroath (1320) had asserted Scottish independence, and by 1323, Bruce was in a strong negotiating position. However, no major military engagement is specifically documented for 1323.

sources.name: Scottish chronicles (e.g., Barbour's The Brus, written later)

events.certainty_note: Robert the Bruce's reign and consolidation of Scottish independence are well-documented, but specific events in 1323 are not prominently recorded.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Mansa Musa's hajj is disputed between 1323 and 1324 CE due to Islamic calendar conversion. No single definitive event of major historical significance is uniquely and unambiguously dated to 1323 CE in global records. The Avignon Papacy and Scottish independence are ongoing processes rather than discrete events of 1323. This year is poorly documented compared to surrounding years.
historiographic_note: 1323 CE is a sparsely documented year in global history. Most major events are either continuations of longer processes (Avignon Papacy, Scottish independence, Ottoman expansion) or are dated ambiguously (Mansa Musa's hajj). European chronicles are available but often focus on political intrigue rather than major events. Islamic sources are limited. Chinese, African, and Asian sources are sparse for this specific year. The historical record for 1323 is thin and biased toward European and Islamic regions.

graph_edges.note: Mansa Musa's hajj may have occurred in either 1323 or 1324 CE; the Islamic calendar conversion creates ambiguity
Continuation of papal residence in Avignon
